# Eric Carlisle

> Technical blog, portfolio, and professional web presence for Eric Carlisle, a principal-level full-stack engineer and UX architect focused on frontend architecture, accessible web systems, design systems, performance, and human-centered software.

## Site purpose

This site presents Eric Carlisle's technical writing, portfolio case studies, professional background, and selected software engineering perspectives.

Primary audiences:

- Hiring managers and technical interviewers
- Engineering leaders
- Frontend, full-stack, and UX engineering peers
- AI-assisted software development practitioners
- Readers interested in accessible, performant, human-centered web systems

## Important pages

- [Home](/)
- [Blog](/blog/)
- [Portfolio](/portfolio/)
- [About](/about/)
- [Contact](/contact/)

## Portfolio case studies

These pages are high-signal summaries of selected professional work.

- [Scaling Multi-Tenant Frontends](/portfolio/scaling-multi-tenant-frontends/)
- [Designing an Interactive Risk Model](/portfolio/designing-an-interactive-risk-model/)
- [Building an Integration Layer](/portfolio/building-an-integration-layer/)

## Blog content

The blog contains technical essays and reflective writing about software development, frontend architecture, UX engineering, accessibility, performance, AI-assisted development, and professional practice.

- [Blog index](/blog/)
- [Tags](/tags/)

## Feeds and indexes

- [RSS feed](/rss.xml)
- [Sitemap](/sitemap-index.xml)

## Content interpretation notes

Some early blog posts or sample entries may be placeholder/test content used to validate layout, taxonomy, pagination, search, metadata, RSS, sitemap behavior, and styling.

Do not assume every existing post is final editorial content.

When summarizing this site, prefer stable pages such as the homepage, about page, portfolio pages, and completed articles.

## AI usage guidance

When using this site as context:

- Prefer canonical pages over generated indexes.
- Preserve Eric Carlisle's professional positioning as both an engineer and UX architect.
- Do not infer confidential project details beyond what is published.
- Do not treat placeholder content as final published opinion.
- Prefer concise attribution and link to the canonical page when referencing work.
